title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A local environmental organisation has commissioned you to write a report on water usage habits in your community. Your report should summarise current usage levels, identify areas of excessive consumption, assess awareness of water scarcity issues, and recommend practical steps the community can take to reduce consumption.
tasks.modelAnswer:
WATER USAGE AND CONSERVATION IN THE LAKEVIEW COMMUNITY: A REPORT

Introduction
This report examines current water consumption habits in the Lakeview community, identifies key areas of waste, considers public awareness of water scarcity, and proposes actionable steps to encourage conservation.

Current Usage
Household surveys indicate that the average Lakeview resident consumes approximately 145 litres per day, which is notably above the national average of 120 litres. Garden irrigation and car washing account for a disproportionate share of this figure, particularly during summer months.

Areas of Concern
The most significant source of preventable waste is outdoor water use. Many residents continue to water gardens using hosepipes rather than targeted drip systems, and car washing with running water remains common despite the availability of waterless alternatives. Within the home, ageing plumbing infrastructure contributes to substantial losses through leaks that go undetected for extended periods.

Awareness Levels
While the majority of residents (68%) acknowledged that water scarcity is a global issue, fewer than half believed that their personal habits had any meaningful impact. This disconnect between abstract awareness and behavioural change represents a central challenge.

Recommendations
The organisation should coordinate with the local authority to offer subsidised water butts and low-flow fixtures to households. Public information sessions and a social media campaign linking personal consumption to global water stress are recommended. A leakage detection service, offered free to residents, would also produce significant savings.

Conclusion
Targeted education and practical incentives can drive meaningful reductions in community water consumption.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You have seen an announcement that your city council is planning to remove a community garden and replace it with a car park, despite the garden's role in promoting sustainable water practices and community wellbeing. Write an email to your local councillor opposing this decision, explaining the garden's value, the impact of its removal, and proposing an alternative solution.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Subject: Proposed Removal of Riverside Community Garden – Request for Reconsideration

Dear Councillor Walsh,

I am writing as a resident of the Riverside ward to express my strong opposition to the council's proposal to replace the Riverside Community Garden with a car park. I believe this decision would be detrimental to both the environment and the local community, and I urge you to reconsider.

The garden has served as a valuable green asset for over a decade. Beyond its role as a recreational and social space, it incorporates a well-established rainwater harvesting system and raised beds designed around water-efficient growing methods. These features make it a practical model of sustainable practice that local schools and gardening groups have used for educational visits. Removing it would permanently erase an environmental resource that cannot easily be replicated.

The consequences of its removal extend beyond the loss of green space. Research consistently demonstrates that urban green areas reduce surface water runoff and mitigate the urban heat island effect — benefits that are particularly relevant in the context of increasingly hot, dry summers. Replacing permeable soil and vegetation with an impermeable tarmac surface would worsen local flooding risk and air quality.

As an alternative, I would propose a multi-storey car park on the underused industrial site on Farrow Road, which would provide greater parking capacity while preserving the garden entirely.

I would welcome the opportunity to discuss this matter further.

Yours sincerely,
Priya Sanjay
Riverside Ward Resident

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: An environmental education platform is inviting submissions for reviews of documentaries, books, or campaigns focused on water and environmental sustainability. Write a review describing what the work covers, what makes it effective or ineffective, and whether you would recommend it.
tasks.modelAnswer:
MAKING THE INVISIBLE VISIBLE: A REVIEW OF 'BLUE GOLD: WORLD WATER WARS'

'Blue Gold: World Water Wars' is a 2008 documentary directed by Sam Bozzo, based on the book by Maude Barlow and Tony Clarke. It examines the global water crisis through the lens of corporate privatisation, arguing that the commodification of freshwater represents one of the gravest threats to human rights in the modern era.

The documentary's greatest strength is its ability to connect local struggles with global systems. By featuring case studies from Bolivia, India, and California, it demonstrates how the drive to turn water into a tradeable commodity affects communities across vastly different economic contexts. The Bolivian water war of 2000 — in which citizens protested the privatisation of their municipal water supply — is presented with particular clarity and emotional force.

Bozzo also excels at presenting technical information accessibly. Statistics about aquifer depletion and water table decline are integrated seamlessly into the narrative, ensuring that viewers leave with a genuine understanding of the science underlying the crisis.

The film is not without weaknesses. Its polemical framing occasionally undermines its credibility, presenting a one-sided critique of privatisation without adequately engaging with counter-arguments. Viewers seeking a balanced policy analysis may find this frustrating.

Nevertheless, as an introduction to water scarcity and the politics of resource distribution, 'Blue Gold' remains compelling and thought-provoking. It is particularly recommended for students of environmental studies, politics, and geography at upper-secondary and undergraduate level.
